feed.title: ale627
feed.subtitle: I'm an analyst, sailor, & trader near SF. ghpages+jekyll+minima+arimo/inconsolata

entry.title: What is important?
entry.content:
What is important to you? Is anything important to you?

Not everyone has the same priorities in life. Some people don’t have priorities. Most people do have priorities but they are flimsy, because they’re made on-the-fly or as a result of circumstances.

Flimsy priorities lead to fuzzy decision making, meaning you might do different things under the same circumstances. The only long-term output from this sort of process is randomness.

Therefore it’s important to define your priorities in a robust way, even if those priorities are to relax 24/7 and never wear shoes. It’s important to decide and know why you decided, to avoid regret later in life.

Here’s the scary thing. No one else cares. They’re too busy trying to do their own thing. Even licensed professionals and family members will only be able to provide advice from their point of view, through their personal filter of experiences and biases.

Make life quality your #1 life mission. It’s up to you to decide on and get what you want in life. How do you decide on what you want? The kind of person you are informs your priorities.

  • What’s your mix?
  • Present happiness
  • Future success
  • Putting it together
  • Ego

What’s your mix?

All people exist along a spectrum of time orientation. Out of 100%, you are part living in the “now” and part in the future. Tied to this orientation are several more concrete characteristics that may help you decide. Think hippy on one hand, and financial analyst on the other.

Are you more rational and logical, or emotional and intuitive? Do you seek comfort and happiness, or growth and achievement? Are you whimsical, or deliberate?

Present Future
Emotional Rational
Intuitive Logical
Comfort Growth
Happiness Achievement
Whimsical Deliberate

  • If you live in the “now”, you prioritize happiness. Happiness, or the absence of stress and anxiety, comes from matching your surroundings to your inner self. It means doing what you want.

  • If you live for the future, you prioritize success. Success, or the accomplishment of goals, is the result of delayed gratification and persistent effort to affect an outcome. It means doing what must be done.

Present happiness and future success stand opposed to each other and represent a trade-off. More of one means less of the other. So what is your desired mix of happiness and achievement? 50/50? 70/30?

Can you define what happiness and achievement looks like to you?

Present happiness

To define your happiness, you need to first know your self. Have you spent time introspectively examining the sources of your identity? The sources are “Nature” and “Nurture”. Within each source are several factors.

Nurture:

  • Place of birth
  • Upbringing
  • School years
  • Foods
  • Culture

Nature:

  • Thought patterns
  • Vices
  • Favorite activities
  • Personality type
  • Spiritual beliefs
  • Carnal desires

These identity factors all affect your happiness. Can you recall times you were very happy? What was happening?

If you’re a tough guy and absolutely loved going to the petting zoo, then if you want to be happy you better schedule some petting time.

If you grew up in Asia and moved to the Midwest, some authentic Dim Sum would likely go a long way to improving your happiness.

Who you think you are is not necessarily who you actually are. Don’t let yourself be so focused on seeming normal that you neglect to actually be happy. This is way too common.

Future success

What are your goals? As a young person, it’s important to think deliberately about what you want to do. There are 80 years in life, and about 50 weeks a year, so there are 4000 weeks in life.

Why do you want to succeed at anything at all? Why be anything or do anything, when you could more easily be and do nothing? It is in the human spirit to continually reach for richer experience.

Many goals require years of specific and concentrated effort and are mutually exclusive with other goals. You can’t achieve them all, so which goals are the most important to you?

There are different types of goals.

  • Physical feats
  • Social connections
  • Money accumulation
  • Things to own
  • Things to do

Once you have defined and prioritized your goals, you can build a gameplan.

Warren Buffett 25-5 rule has you list your 25 (career) goals, then circle the top 5 and disregard the bottom 20 thereafter. With this handful of goals, you can construct 1, 3, 5, and 10 year plans with action items relating your current situation to your desired situation.

This framework allows clarity of thought. With action items in mind, your subconscious can:

  • Spend time wrestling with these goals to arrive at novel solutions (the AHA! moment in the shower)
  • Monitor your life for in-roads that shortcut the action item.

This is how you get lucky. There’s a ton of time.

Putting it together

Identifying your priority mix and delineating what happiness and success look like lets you troubleshoot your lifestyle more accurately than a therapist.

Given that you know what makes you happy, if you feel anxiety or stress, it means you are living too much in the future. Do things that stoke your true identity.

Given that you know what to do to succeed, if you feel like you’re spinning your wheels, it means you are living too much in the now. Consult your established goals.

Ego

I’ve given tips for identifying and getting your most personal desires.

Desires, whether short-term happiness or long-term accomplishments, arise out of our ego. Getting what you want fufills the ego.

You may notice that your wants are fleeting. As soon as you get something, you want something else. To this end, the only way out of the rat race is to stop wanting. You stop wanting by killing your ego.

The ultimate objective is to free yourself of ego. Without ego, you are able to interact with the world without a filter of perception, seeing and experiencing things as they truly are. This real interaction is the truest form of living, as it is open to actual reality. Do you dare jettison the basis of your current happiness and future goals, in pursuit of an objective truth? Whether that is something you are interested in is something to decide for yourself.
entry.summary: What is important to you? Is anything important to you?

entry.title: NHL preseason in a suite
entry.content:
I got the last minute invite to join Brian with an extra ticket to the suites at the Sharks preseason NHL game last night. His cousin Eric works at MicroSemi, a semiconductor company. MicroSemi has a suite at SAP Center.

We joined a gathering of NVIDIA and MicroSemi folks who work together. San Jose Sharks vs the brand spankin’ new Golden Knights from Las Vegas.

When a new team like the Golden Knights is chartered - one MicroSemi guy explained - instead of only being able to hire from the free agent pool, each existing team reserves a certain number of players, then the new team selects some unreserved players from each team for their roster.

Catering provided beer and wine, hotdogs, sliders, and cookies. Of course nothing is free and everything is overpriced but MicroSemi set us up well. The suites, which run around the whole stadium, are all in the nosebleed section, but you can see the puck at all times due to the angle of the seating and the rink lighting.

The game was exciting with 7 goals total. No scores in the first period, with 4 in a row by San Jose in the 2nd. At this point I admit to betting Brian $5 on the Knights scoring.

Final score San Jose 5 - Las Vegas 2.
Good fun! Thanks again to Brian, Eric and MicroSemi.
entry.summary: I got the last minute invite to join Brian with an extra ticket to the suites at the Sharks preseason NHL game last night. His cousin Eric works at MicroSemi, a semiconductor company. MicroSemi has a suite at SAP Center.

entry.title: Van Damme camping
entry.content:
Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

Jake, Brian, and I camped at Van Damme State Park Friday and Saturday night. (Van Damme, gurl)

It was a 5hr drive from San Jose in rush hour traffic! Luckily we did take the scenic routes closer to the coast, which ended up being further, but without traffic and along 2 lane roads. As Google Maps tends to adjust ETA adding up to half an hour as traffic rises, we definitely saved time thinking through our route.

The site was #37 and was situated nicely on top of a rise, with bathrooms nearby and good sunlight in the morning.

We kept meals to a couple ingredients to save space and reduce costs:

  • Pasta (tortellini, alfredo)
  • Smores (graham crackers, marshmallows, hershey’s)
  • Quesadillas (tortillas, mexican cheese, sausage)
  • Oatmeal (oats, peanut butter)
  • Mandarin Oranges

Our original plan was to take a sea cave kayaking tour, but it was expensive and full. So we called an audible and drove up to Fort Bragg. Here’s the places we went:

  • Glass Beach, to see the glass and swim. Cold water filled with kelp forests! The Van Damme camp ranger told us Glass Beach used to be a literal dump, then the county was told to clean it up. All the broken bottles became glass beach. Now that’s good marketing. We talked to an abalone diver who was tagging some shells, and he explained he was part of a competition taking place today.

  • Caspar Beach, where a Triton Free Diving competition weigh in was going on. We saw abalone, cod, and scallops the size of a baseball mitt.

  • North Coast Brewery, home of Brother Thelonius Belgian Style Abbey Ale (appley) and the Rasputin Russian Imperial Stout (chocolatey).

  • Pygmy Forest Trail. Pygmy means short, and this forest’s stunted growth is caused by poor soil, as the area sat underwater many years ago. There were huckleberrys (very edible!) along the boardwalk through the pygmy forest, followed by a wide fire trail to the next intersection about 2 1/2 miles up, surrounded by redwoods.

  • Big River beach, to enjoy a beer and catch the sunset after the hike. The sun sets over the point instead of the ocean, so you get to enjoy the flash of the sun setting, then a few extra minutes of light to finish your beer as the actual sunset occurs. The area in the back, a broad beach along the big river, looked excellent for hanging out during the day. (noted)

  • Russian River Brewery, home of Pliny the Elder/Younger. A great pit stop on the way back. It was very busy with locals on Sunday as it is happy hour all day, so we ate down the street at a burger joint and came back. I tried the Golden Ale and grabbed a growler of Pliny.
entry.summary: Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

entry.title: Finless Foods fun
entry.content:
I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

Zois -my housemate Ian’s friend from time in Chile- stayed on our couch for a few days. He invited me to Massive Attack at 1015 Folsom with Emily, a friend visiting SF. Emily was staying in SoMa with Mike, who she met in Taiwan.

Mike lives with Brian, and together they run Finless Foods, a new food startup creating fish without fishing or farming. They’re growing fish meat from stem cells. Finless is creating surimi (fish paste) in the lab now.

  • Their fish is healthier than caught fish which contains mercury and plastic.
  • It is also healthier than farmed fish which contains antibiotics and hormones.
  • As the fish is grown without a body of water, production can be geographically distributed.
  • Since no fish are harmed, Finless appeals to ethical vegans.

An interesting question my housemate Ian asked is: can this fish be produced in a way that is less carbon intensive than the usual process?

When Zois and I picked up Mike, Brian, and co-worker Jihyun, they had just wrapped up an initial tasting. “This is three quarters of the company in the backseat, right now!” they joked as they piled in. Mike was looking forward to “only” having to speak in front of 1000 people the following week. Easy, in comparison.

Zois, Emily, Mike, and I went to Massive Attack together. It was a fun show, closing with “Teardrop”. Afterwards, we took a peek at the IndieBio incubator which was right around the corner.

In the morning, Mike mentioned that he had actually felt unstressed for the first time in months. Balance is important! (An hour later he was off to New York on a plane.)

Here’s the link to the “1000 people” talk mentioned above - IndieBio SF Demo Day 5.

Mike comes on just before the 1:03:00 mark.

Good luck to Finless Foods!!
entry.summary: I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

entry.title: Why do blogs die?
entry.content:
Blogs are everywhere. You probably have an old one under your bed somewhere.

They’re started by teachers, travellers, investors, product people and developers.

They’re hosted on Blogger, Wordpress, or Tumblr. Or on Squarespace, Ghost, Wix, Weebly, etc.

They’re painstakenly designed, categorized, and paletted.

Then there’s 5 posts total and the most recent one is from over 2 years ago. It’s tragic really. So much intent and good will, stopped dead in its tracks.

Here’s this person who put in a ton of platform work to connect with the world, and you can just see how quickly it fizzled and died.

Why?

A) People make blogs about stuff they want to be an expert in, not about stuff they’re already experts in. B) Turns out, it’s tiring to try to be something you’re not.

The author is neither writing for themselves nor from actual knowledge. They’re writing to some pie-in-the-sky audience and putting on a persona to attract them.

It gets harder to write over time because there’s no new ideas being discussed, only regurgitation.

This ecosystem of B.S is particularly obvious with LinkedIn published articles lately.

A job seeker mashes his keyboard for a few hours to create a listicle (5 ways to be more productive at work). Upon reading it he thinks to himself in horror, “What did I just write?” Given the huge sunk cost of having written the article, he shrugs and hits SUBMIT, figuring some actual professional will be able to understand what he’s getting at.
Later a reader clicks on and skims through the article, thinking to himself, “What is this that I’m reading?” Confused and wasted, he shrugs and hits LIKE, figuring someone will see his like and think he understood it.

We can all agree the internet doesn’t need any more of that kind of stuff. It’s wasting everyone’s time.

So guess what? This is going to be the exact opposite.

I’m going to write randomly and colloquially, and only about things that I understand and that interest me. There will be no standard length post, and no general topic. Posts will be edited over time.

Buckle up!
entry.summary: Blogs are everywhere. You probably have an old one under your bed somewhere.
